narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 31, your academic profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, along with coming from an upper-middle-class family background and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.5% probability that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction was driven primarily by your third-year school decile ranking, which had the strongest influence and pushed the model firmly toward forecasting failure, likely because being in the bottom decile signals challenges in sustaining high performance over time. Your first-year decile ranking, also at the bottom, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential factor, further tilting the prediction against passing. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the third-most important factor, actually working in your favor by nudging the model slightly toward expecting success, especially since it's right around the typical 3.21 average for applicants. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 31 is notably below the average of 36.70, which didn't help, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your full-time status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%. These early academic struggles in school deciles, well below the averages of about 5.5, outweighed your solid GPA and set you apart from peers who generally rank higher and show consistent upward trajectories. While family income and other details played smaller roles, the model zeroed in on those low decile positions as red flags for bar exam readiness. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your school performance record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could significantly shift your predicted odds, opening doors at this or other programs. You're clearly motivated to pursue law, and with targeted improvements, the model—and admissions committees—will likely see a different story next time.
